questions.question: Why does DBT recommend practicing skills regularly, even on good days?

options.text: Because skills only work if you use them every single day without exception
Because your brain under extreme stress defaults to what is most practiced, so skills rehearsed in calm become instinctive in crisis
Because you should always expect a crisis and be on guard
Because professional therapists require daily practice logs before they will work with you

questions.explanation: Skills are like muscles — they need regular practice to become automatic. When your brain is under extreme emotional stress, it defaults to whatever has been most practiced. If you only use skills in crisis, they will be harder to access when you need them most. Practicing in calm conditions builds the neural pathways that make skills instinctive during high-intensity moments.

questions.question: What are the four components of a comprehensive DBT program?

options.text: Medication management, group therapy, journaling, and yoga
Individual therapy, skills group, phone coaching, and therapist consultation team
Mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation, and interpersonal effectiveness
Assessment, diagnosis, treatment planning, and discharge

questions.explanation: Comprehensive DBT includes four integrated components: individual therapy (weekly sessions with behavioral analysis), skills group (teaching all four modules over about 24 weeks), phone coaching (brief real-time support between sessions), and therapist consultation team (therapists supporting each other to stay effective). Option C describes the four skills modules, not the four program components.

questions.question: Which strategy does the lesson recommend for overcoming the obstacle of forgetting to practice skills?

options.text: Set alarms every hour throughout the day as constant reminders
Only practice when you feel an emotional crisis approaching
Tie practice to an existing habit (habit stacking), such as doing a Wise Mind check-in after pouring your morning coffee
Ask someone else to remind you every day until it becomes automatic

questions.explanation: Habit stacking — linking a new practice to an existing routine — is an evidence-based strategy for building consistent habits. By tying your skill practice to something you already do automatically (like making coffee), you create a reliable cue that makes the new behavior easier to remember and sustain.

questions.question: According to the lesson, what should you look for when choosing a DBT therapist?

options.text: Any therapist who mentions DBT on their website is equally qualified
Ask about formal DBT training, whether they offer all four program components, use diary cards, and participate in a consultation team
Choose the therapist with the most years of general experience, regardless of DBT-specific training
Only work with therapists who guarantee results within the first month

questions.explanation: When seeking a DBT therapist, ask whether they have completed formal DBT training (not just a weekend workshop), whether they offer all four components of DBT or only skills, whether they use diary cards and behavioral chain analysis, and whether they are part of a consultation team. You can also look for DBT-Linehan Board certification at dbt-lbc.org.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the recommended daily DBT practice structure from the lesson?

options.text: A single 60-minute mindfulness session each morning
Morning mindfulness check-in, micro-practices throughout the day, evening emotion log, and weekly skills review
Practice only during emotional crises to ensure skills are used when they matter most
Complete all four DBT modules in a single daily study session

questions.explanation: The recommended structure includes a morning mindfulness check-in (3-5 minutes), micro-practices throughout the day such as body scans and emotion labeling (30-60 seconds each), an evening emotion log (5 minutes), and a weekly skills review (15-20 minutes). Consistency matters more than duration — short, regular practice outperforms long, infrequent sessions.

title: Building a DBT Skills Practice for Life - Quiz
